# How to Design a Page Object Model 
#horizontalDecoupling #verticalSlicing

_Last updated: September 7th, 2024_

_Topics: page object model, page objects, program by wishful thinking, domain specific language layer_

_Major Topics: Behaviour-Driven Design, Design Principles, Design Patterns_

Now that we’ve loaded up our tests, let’s move onto the next layer inward — the _Domain Specific Language Layer._ This is where we’ll define the Page Object Model for the UI and start to express the user interactions we expect to work within the UI.

## Lesson Goals

In this lesson, we will:

- learn the code-first vs. best practice-first approach to building and testing UIs
- understand the page object model and how do you typically design it
- design & build the page object model / DSL layer by wishful thinking for DDDForum

## From Code-First to Best Practice-First: UI testing & design

How do Code-First developers approach testing and building the frontend?

Well, we know from experience going through Code-First that we typically don’t think too much about tests until afterwards. There’s no mystery there.

So how _do_ developers usually tend to build out the frontend?

I ran a poll in our community Discord and it confirmed what I thought to be true.

Most of us don’t start with the tests.

We don’t start with the API integrations.

Most of the time, at Code-First, we start with either the layout or the components.

And that’s not _bad —_ it can be quite good if we build a good **conceptual model of the UI** first.

### Starting w/ a conceptual model of the UI layout

As always, the more we conform to **Abstraction**, the better the design will be.

So for example, if you have the option between:

- _**Component-First →** I see rough vision/screens → components → layout → logic_
- _**Layout First →** I see rough vision/screens → layout → components → logic_

I’d wager that the layout-first version would probably end up with better structure.

For example, if we had 3 pages, we’d aim to identify the commonalities between them, break that into a layout, create the pages and routes, and then the skeleton of the components — then get it looking nice, even if not really functional — but visually accurate.

And you’d typically do this at the React Layer.

For example, for some sort of Job Board website, you might be thinking in terms of the _react router_ and the actual react components that you need to do this.

```tsx
import React from 'react';
import { Router, Route, IndexRoute, browserHistory } from 'react-router';
import Header from './components/Header';
import JobsDetailPage from './pages/JobsDetailPage';
import JobListingsMainPage from './pages/JobListingsMainPage';
import Footer from './components/Footer';

const App = ({ children }) => (
  <div>
    <Header />
    <main>{children}</main>
    <Footer />
  </div>
);

// Routing Configuration
const Routes = () => (
  <Router history={browserHistory}>
    <Route path="/" component={App}>
      <IndexRoute component={JobListingsMainPage} /> {/* Job Listings as the homepage */}
      <Route path="/jobs/:jobId" component={JobsDetailPage} /> {/* Job detail view */}
    </Route>
  </Router>
);

export default Routes;
```

That’s GOOD.

But as we move to Best Practice-First, we want to **raise the conceptual model that we start with and work outside-in/downwards**.

As we work with tests, we want to raise the starting place to the level of the **domain specific language layer — the page object model**.

## What is the page object model?

The page object model is an object-oriented conceptual model of the UI.

Acting as the DSL layer of the 4-tier acceptance test architecture, page objects abstract the user interaction, enabling you to focus on your expression of What Not How.

Here’s what I mean:

```tsx
given('I am a new user', async () => {
  user = new CreateUserInputBuilder()
    .withAllRandomDetails()
    .build();
  
  await pages.registration.open();
  await pages.registration.acceptMarketingEmails();
});

when('I register with valid account details accepting marketing emails', async () => {
  await pages.registration.enterAccountDetails(user);
  await pages.registration.submitRegistrationForm();
});
```

Notice that the language is just _one level lower_ in abstraction than the acceptance test specification code from the Gherkins feature file?

You can take _any_ UI, and as long as you can express what a user **does** on the page in plain English, you can use page objects to program by wishful thinking.

That’s the idea.

### Designing page objects

Designing page objects is similar to what you typically do with React. First, you **start with the conceptual model of the view**.

But instead of going **straight to React Components**, you go towards an **object-oriented expression** of how you’d structure the app instead.

Back to DDDForum.

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

It’s clear that there’s a **layout**.

And within the layout there are **pages**.

There’s a **header** and the concept of **notifications** (toast) as well.

So with all of that said and thought through, it appears the object-oriented or JSON-like structure could be something like this:

```tsx
const app = {
  notifications: new NotificationsComponent(),
  layout: {
    header: new HeaderComponent()
  },
  pages: {
    main: new MainPage(),
    registration: new RegistrationPage()
  }
}
```

And it’s from this **conceptual model** that we can then use in our tests to program by wishful thinking.

Let’s now do this together for the success scenario.

## The Page Object / DSL Layer for DDDForum

### Reminder: the success scenario w/ marketing subscription selected

Let’s recall the success scenario.

You’ll get to the registration page, enter details into the form, select the marketing checkbox, and then submit, get redirected, and see the user details in the header.

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

### Page object testing architecture

Before I dive into this, let me quickly explain the architecture of how we use Page Objects. It's always ideal to have clarity on the architecture of the collaborating objects before you write any code.

- test → creates the page objects → defines the structure of the page objects → each page object has access to puppeteer wrapper → puppeteer wrapper provides access to puppeteer 

## Writing the test

Here’s the scenario we’re going to test.

```gherkin
Scenario: Successful registration with marketing emails accepted
	Given I am a new user
	When I register with valid account details accepting marketing emails
	Then I should be granted access to my account
	And I should expect to receive marketing emails
```

Let’s use the same techniques we always do. Start at the end.

### **1) “I should expect to receive marketing emails”**

What has to happen here?

Well, since we’re validating the _view_ and the _Domain Specific Language_ here is actually the **page objects**, there’s nothing we actually need to verify here.

```tsx
and('I should expect to receive marketing emails', () => {
    // @See backend 
  });
```

You know what’s great?

Because we have the same acceptance test, this is something that could, would, and _do_ actually verify on the backend with a different type of test.

Same structure, different content.

Look. Remember this from the backend E2e test?

```tsx
// packages/backend/tests/features/registration.e2e.ts

and('I should expect to receive marketing emails', () => {
  expect(addEmailToListResponse).toBeTruthy();
});
```

And once we get to “Advanced Testing”, we’ll be able to take that even further with **test doubles** as **spies** in our High Value Unit Tests.

```tsx
and('I should expect to receive marketing emails', () => {
  expect(addEmailToListResponse).toBeTruthy();
  expect(contactListAPISpy.getTimesMethodCalled('addEmailToList')).toEqual(1);
});
```

But we’ll get there when we get there.

Next up.

### **2) “I should be granted access to my account”**

How might we detect this?

Well if you recall, once you successfully register, you’ll get redirected to the main page. Following that, you should see your username in the header.

So then the page object model could look like what follows:

```tsx
then('I should be granted access to my account', async () => {
  expect(await layout.header.getLoggedInUserName()).toContain(userInput.username);
});
```

### **3) when “I** register with valid account details accepting marketing emails"

As for the actual registration itself, this is relatively straightforward.

We have a form to submit, and we also have the marketing checkbox. This might not yet exist in the view, but it doesn’t matter. We’re just focused on expressing What, not How.

```tsx
when('I register with valid account details accepting marketing emails', async () => {
  await pages.registrationPage.enterAccountDetails(userInput);
  await pages.registrationPage.acceptMarketingEmails();
  await pages.registrationPage.submitRegistrationForm();
});
```

### 4) given “I am a new user”

And then again finally, the given — how do we set up the new user?

Take a look at how we’re doing it here and I’ll call a few things out

```tsx
given('I am a new user', async () => {
  userInput = new CreateUserInputBuilder()
    .withAllRandomDetails()
    .build();
  
  await pages.registrationPage.open();
});
```

We can re-use the userInputBuilder from the shared module.

And with the design of “**registrationPage**.open” — well, this is a bit of a convention I use for page objects to get them to all conform to a simple structure in a page object base class.

### Using a page object base class

Why bother with a base class?

Well, I already know in advance that I’m going to have a lot of different PageObjects to support many different pages, so this acts as an API standard to hide the lower level details, which would look like what follows.

```tsx
import { PuppeteerPageDriver } from "../driver";

export abstract class PageObject {
  protected driver: PuppeteerPageDriver;
  public url: string;

  constructor (driver: PuppeteerPageDriver, url: string) {
    this.driver = driver;
    this.url = url;
  }

  public async open () {
    await this.driver.page.goto(this.url);
  }
}
```

Getting a bit ahead of myself here before the next lesson, but this should give you some more clarity behind the design decision.

### What the end result looks like

```tsx
import { defineFeature, loadFeature } from 'jest-cucumber';
import { sharedTestRoot } from '@dddforum/shared/src/paths';
import { CreateUserInputBuilder } from "@dddforum/shared/tests/support/builders/createUserBuilder";
import { CreateUserInput } from "@dddforum/shared/src/api/users";
import { Pages } from '../support/pages/pages';

import * as path from 'path';
import { PuppeteerPageDriver } from '../support/driver';
import { App, createApplicationPageObject } from '../support/pages';
import { DatabaseFixture } from '@dddforum/shared/tests/support/fixtures/databaseFixture';

const feature = loadFeature(path.join(sharedTestRoot, 'features/registration.feature'), { tagFilter: '@frontend' });

defineFeature(feature, (test) => {
  let app: App
  let pages: Pages;
  let puppeteerPageDriver: PuppeteerPageDriver;
  let userInput: CreateUserInput;
  let databaseFixture: DatabaseFixture

  beforeAll(async () => {
    databaseFixture = new DatabaseFixture();
    puppeteerPageDriver = await PuppeteerPageDriver.create({ 
      headless: false,
      slowMo: 50,
    });
    app = createApplicationPageObject(puppeteerPageDriver);
    pages = app.pages;
  });

  afterAll(async () => {
    await puppeteerPageDriver.browser.close();
  });

  afterEach(async () => {
    await databaseFixture.resetDatabase();
  });

  // Need to put timeout here.
  jest.setTimeout(60000);
  
  test('Successful registration with marketing emails accepted', ({ given, when, then, and }) => {

    given('I am a new user', async () => {
      user = new CreateUserInputBuilder()
        .withAllRandomDetails()
        .build();
      
      await pages.registration.open();
      await pages.registration.acceptMarketingEmails();
    });

    when('I register with valid account details accepting marketing emails', async () => {
      await pages.registration.enterAccountDetails(userInput);
      await pages.registration.submitRegistrationForm();
    });

    then('I should be granted access to my account', async () => {
      expect(await app.layout.header.getLoggedInUserName()).toContain(userInput.username);
    });

    and('I should expect to receive marketing emails', () => {
      // @See backend 
    });
  });
```

## Final details

### Expressing everything upfront

You’ll notice that we’ve already paid mention to things like the `PuppeteerPageDriver` and so on. That’s right. When I write these tests, it’s important to have a clear picture of what the architecture looks like **in advance** so that you can focus on expressing the APIs of the architecture.

So many times, I’ve tried to write tests, programming by wishful thinking in reverse, without clarity on what the essential collaborating objects actually were.

Those tests never worked well, and the design was always poor.

You need to know what objects you’re dealing with in advance.

This is what the systems thinking model I’ve created for myself, _FA²STR_ goes Find → Architect → Automate → Specify-Test-Refine. Architect always comes first.

### Creating the page objects

How are we actually creating the page objects?

I’ve written a small factory function to piece it all together.

Here’s what that could look like.

```css
import { AppNotifications, HeaderComponent  } from "../components";
import { PuppeteerPageDriver } from "../driver";
import { RegistrationPage } from "./registrationPage";

// Define the structure
export interface App {
  pages: Pages;
  layout: {
    header: HeaderComponent;
  },
  notifications: AppNotifications;
}

interface Pages {
  registration: RegistrationPage;
}

export function createAppObject(pageDriver: PuppeteerPageDriver): App {  
  return {
    pages: {
      registration: new RegistrationPage(pageDriver)
    },
    layout: {
      header: new HeaderComponent(pageDriver),
    },
    notifications: new AppNotifications(pageDriver)
  }
}
```

## Your Turn!

Your turn to set up your page objects and the failing test.

**Specifically, make sure you’ve set up the following.**

✅  I have designed pageObjects that represent the conceptual model of the Ui using programming by wishful thinking

✅  I am making use of shared builders.

✅ I have re-organized and made use of a shared database fixture to control the state of my tests

## FAQ

### “This seems like so much work!”

Yes. Sometimes I’d just prefer to build the view first, and that’s actually okay. But just understand the trade-offs. Metaphysically, the phenomenon we’re dealing with is:

- _upfront vs. emergent_
- _top down vs. bottom up_
- _division vs. unity_
- _divergence vs. convergence_
- _yin vs. yang_

Nothing exists in separation.

All good. Just understand the relationship here.

As long as you’re aware of what’s going on, you can decide if you want to start from the very top with page objects and let that influence the design as you work inwards…

OR you can just build the view and then zoom out and conceptualize it again from a level of abstraction higher, **knowing that** you may very well end up re-adjusting the structure of your UI to conform to the **containing shape of your page object model**.

I don’t make the rules. That’s just how it is.

## Summary

- It’s likely that you already create a conceptual model of the UI in your head when you build out your views in React.
- The page object model is an object-oriented conceptual model of the UI, programmed by wishful thinking
- Acting as the DSL layer of the 4-tier acceptance test architecture, page objects abstract the user interaction, enabling you to focus on your expression of What Not How.
- To design page objects, follow your typical process of abstracting the UI into common parts; what’s most common is the concept of _layouts, pages_ and _components/pageElements_
